sections.plain_summary: Sorbitol and polyol intolerance is a digestive condition where the body has difficulty absorbing certain sweeteners and sugar alcohols found in many foods and products. This leads to bloating, gas, diarrhoea, and abdominal discomfort after eating affected foods. It is not an allergy, but rather a problem with how the intestines process these substances.
sections.detailed_summary: Sorbitol and polyol intolerance occurs when the small intestine cannot adequately absorb polyols—a group of sugar alcohols that includes sorbitol, xylitol, maltitol, and mannitol. These are commonly used as artificial sweeteners in diet foods, sugar-free products, and some fruits. When polyols reach the colon unabsorbed, they draw water in osmotically and are fermented by bacteria, producing gas, bloating, and changes in bowel habit. The condition is thought to be a form of carbohydrate malabsorption, similar to lactose intolerance or fructose malabsorption. It may coexist with other functional digestive conditions and is not caused by immune system activation.

prevalence.summary: Sorbitol and polyol intolerance is common but exact prevalence is unclear—likely affects a significant minority of the population.
prevalence.estimate: Estimated 5–20% of the general population, possibly higher in people with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
prevalence.notes: Prevalence estimates vary widely because the condition is often underdiagnosed and not routinely tested. It overlaps with IBS and FODMAP sensitivity. No large-scale epidemiological studies have precisely defined prevalence by region or age group.

subgroups.name: Primary polyol intolerance
subgroups.description: Reduced capacity to absorb sorbitol and other polyols due to low expression of sorbitol transporter (GLUT5) or other genetic factors. Symptoms occur with relatively small doses of polyols.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms triggered by small amounts of sorbitol or sugar-free products
Occurs independently of other food intolerances
May be lifelong and relatively stable

subgroups.name: Secondary or stress-related polyol intolerance
subgroups.description: Reduced absorption capacity triggered or worsened by gut inflammation, dysbiosis, high stress, or concurrent infection. Severity may fluctuate.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms worse during or after illness, or during high-stress periods
May improve with treatment of underlying inflammation or dysbiosis
Often coexists with IBS or other functional disorders

subgroups.name: Polyol intolerance as part of FODMAP sensitivity
subgroups.description: Difficulty absorbing polyols as one component of broader low-FODMAP intolerance, which also includes problems with excess fructose, lactose, or fructans.
distinguishing_features: Triggered by multiple FODMAP foods, not just polyols
May improve significantly with low-FODMAP diet

symptoms.notes: Often appears within 30 minutes to 2 hours of eating polyol-containing foods; may worsen through the day.
Can range from mild to severe; often relieved by defecation or passing gas.
Results from bacterial fermentation of unabsorbed polyols in the colon.
Due to osmotic effect of polyols drawing water into the bowel; may be the primary symptom for some people.
Less common but can occur in some individuals, particularly if polyol intake is low and other factors are involved.
May accompany severe bloating or cramping; usually mild.
May result from dysbiosis, malabsorption, or reaction to fermentation byproducts; more common during flares.

key_patterns: Symptoms appear 30 minutes to 2 hours after eating sugar-free products, artificial sweeteners, or certain fruits (especially stone fruits and pears).
Bloating and gas are relieved by bowel movements or passing flatus.
Symptoms improve within days of avoiding sorbitol and polyols.
Symptoms worsen on days with multiple sugar-free or low-calorie products; improve on regular foods.
Diarrhoea is most common after meals containing polyols; constipation is less typical.
No symptoms with naturally sweet foods like honey, ripe bananas, or regular sugar (unless other intolerances exist).

at_home.name: Elimination and rechallenge diet
at_home.description: Remove all known polyol sources for 3–5 days and observe symptoms. Then reintroduce one polyol-containing food at a time in small amounts, waiting 2 hours and noting any response. Repeat with different foods on separate days to identify which polyols trigger symptoms.
at_home.safety_notes: Safe to do at home. If symptoms are severe, spread challenges over a longer period. If you have multiple food intolerances, elimination of only polyols may not fully clarify the picture; consider working with a dietitian.

at_home.name: Food and symptom diary
at_home.description: Record all foods, drinks, and timing, along with symptom onset, type, and severity. Review after 1–2 weeks to identify patterns. Include context like stress, sleep, and menstrual cycle if relevant.
at_home.safety_notes: Non-invasive and informative. Can be time-consuming but is often more revealing than clinical tests.

clinical.name: Hydrogen and methane breath test (after sorbitol challenge)
clinical.description: Patient fasts overnight and then consumes a measured dose of sorbitol (typically 10–25 g). Breath samples are collected at baseline and at 30-minute intervals over 2–3 hours. High levels of hydrogen or methane indicate sorbitol malabsorption. A positive test strongly supports the diagnosis.
clinical.availability: Offered by gastroenterologists and digestive health clinics. Less commonly available than lactose or fructose breath tests but increasingly used for FODMAP assessment.

clinical.name: Hydrogen and methane breath test (after mannitol or xylitol)
clinical.description: Similar to sorbitol breath test but using mannitol or xylitol as the challenge agent. Different polyols may be absorbed differently by individual patients, so testing with multiple polyols may be informative.
clinical.availability: Less commonly ordered than sorbitol testing but available at specialized gastroenterology or functional medicine clinics.

clinical.name: Stool test for markers of dysbiosis
clinical.description: Measures bacterial composition, diversity, and fermentation byproducts (short-chain fatty acids, phenols). May reveal dysbiosis that worsens polyol intolerance, though not diagnostic for polyol malabsorption itself.
clinical.availability: Offered by functional medicine practitioners and some gastroenterologists. Not routine NHS practice; often requires private testing.

clinical.name: Genetic testing for GLUT5 transporter variants
clinical.description: Tests for genetic variations in the sorbitol transporter gene (SLC5A1). May identify individuals with genetic predisposition to polyol malabsorption, but clinical utility is still being established.
clinical.availability: Research-level or offered through specialized functional medicine clinics. Not widely available or routinely recommended for clinical diagnosis.

related_conditions.name: Fructose malabsorption
related_conditions.explanation: Both are carbohydrate malabsorption disorders with similar symptoms. Fructose intolerance is often tested alongside polyol sensitivity. Some people have both conditions and must avoid both high-fructose and high-polyol foods.

related_conditions.name: Lactose intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Another form of sugar malabsorption causing similar bloating, gas, and diarrhoea. People with one carbohydrate intolerance often have others. Distinguishing between them requires targeted dietary trials or breath testing.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: Polyol intolerance is common in people with IBS and may account for some of their symptoms. FODMAP restriction (which includes polyol avoidance) is an evidence-based treatment for IBS.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: Dysbiosis and bacterial overgrowth increase fermentation of unabsorbed polyols, worsening bloating and gas. Treating SIBO may reduce polyol intolerance symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: Polyol intolerance may coexist with upper digestive dysfunction, causing early fullness, nausea, and discomfort alongside lower GI symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Dysbiosis or microbial imbalance
related_conditions.explanation: Polyol fermentation can favour certain bacteria over others, potentially driving dysbiosis. Conversely, dysbiosis worsens the intolerance by increasing gas-producing bacteria.

related_conditions.name: Intestinal permeability (leaky gut)
related_conditions.explanation: Fermentation byproducts and dysbiosis may increase intestinal permeability, potentially worsening symptoms and cross-talk with other food intolerances.

strategies.name: Strict polyol avoidance
strategies.description: Remove sorbitol, xylitol, maltitol, mannitol, and isomalt from the diet. This means avoiding sugar-free sweets, diet drinks, artificial sweeteners, certain fruits (pears, stone fruits, watermelon), mushrooms, cauliflower, and some vegetables. Read food labels carefully. Most people see significant symptom improvement within 3–7 days.
strategies.notes: This is the primary and most effective treatment. However, complete avoidance can be socially limiting. Some people tolerate small amounts if spread throughout the day. Once symptoms resolve, cautious reintroduction can help identify individual tolerance thresholds.

strategies.name: Low-FODMAP diet
strategies.description: A structured, evidence-based diet that limits fermentable carbohydrates, including polyols, excess fructose, lactose, and fructans. Usually overseen by a registered dietitian. Typically done in three phases: strict elimination (2–6 weeks), systematic reintroduction (8–12 weeks), and personalised maintenance.
strategies.notes: Most effective for IBS-related polyol intolerance. Requires professional guidance to ensure nutritional adequacy and to identify which foods are genuinely problematic. Not necessary for everyone with polyol intolerance alone.

strategies.name: Gradual reintroduction and threshold mapping
strategies.description: After symptoms resolve (after 2–4 weeks of avoidance), slowly reintroduce polyol-containing foods in small amounts to identify individual tolerance levels. Some people can tolerate small portions of polyols if they avoid other triggers. Record amounts and symptoms to create a personal 'tolerance map'.
strategies.notes: Helps distinguish between complete intolerance and dose-dependent sensitivity. Not all polyols are equally problematic for each person.

strategies.name: Spacing meals and eating slowly
strategies.description: Eat smaller, more frequent meals and chew food thoroughly. This slows gastric emptying and reduces the bolus of unabsorbed carbohydrates entering the colon at once, potentially reducing bloating and pain.
strategies.notes: Simple and can provide mild symptom relief, especially for bloating. Does not eliminate the problem if polyol intake is high.

strategies.name: Adequate hydration
strategies.description: Drink sufficient water throughout the day. Dehydration can worsen constipation and concentrate fermentation byproducts, increasing bloating.
strategies.notes: General health measure; does not directly address polyol malabsorption but supports digestive function.

strategies.name: Regular physical activity
strategies.description: Gentle to moderate exercise (walking, yoga, swimming) can promote gut motility and reduce bloating. Vigorous exercise during a flare may worsen symptoms.
strategies.notes: Helps with overall IBS management and gas clearance. Benefits vary by individual.

strategies.name: Stress reduction and sleep
strategies.description: Stress and poor sleep worsen gut permeability, dysbiosis, and intolerance symptoms. Practise relaxation, meditation, or mindfulness; prioritise 7–9 hours of sleep.
strategies.notes: Addresses a key modifiable driver of secondary polyol intolerance. Can significantly reduce symptoms when combined with dietary avoidance.

strategies.name: Peppermint oil (enteric-coated capsules)
strategies.description: Peppermint oil relaxes intestinal smooth muscle and may reduce bloating, cramping, and pain. Enteric-coated capsules are designed to release in the small intestine.
strategies.notes: Evidence is stronger for IBS than specifically for polyol intolerance, but often helpful as symptomatic relief. Take 30–60 minutes before meals. Avoid if you have acid reflux.

strategies.name: Digestive enzymes (non-systemic)
strategies.description: Some formulations claim to aid the digestion of sugar alcohols, though evidence is limited. Protease and amylase may help with overall carbohydrate digestion.
strategies.notes: No specific enzyme breaks down sorbitol or xylitol. May offer placebo benefit or mild general support; avoidance remains the primary approach.

strategies.name: Probiotics and prebiotics (selected)
strategies.description: Some people report symptom improvement with specific probiotic strains (e.g., Bifidobacterium or Lactobacillus) that ferment carbohydrates less vigorously. Prebiotics may worsen symptoms during active intolerance.
strategies.notes: Evidence is mixed. Probiotics may help secondary intolerance but are unlikely to resolve primary intolerance. Avoid inulin and FOS-based prebiotics if polyol-sensitive. Work with a practitioner to select appropriate strains.

strategies.name: Pancreatic digestive enzyme supplementation (if needed)
strategies.description: For people with concurrent pancreatic insufficiency or severe dysbiosis, pancreatic enzyme supplements may improve overall digestion and reduce fermentation load.
strategies.notes: Only relevant if pancreatic insufficiency is confirmed. Not standard for isolated polyol intolerance.

strategies.name: Antispasmodic medications (short-term)
strategies.description: Prescription medications like dicycloverine or hyoscyamine relax intestinal muscle and can provide short-term relief of cramping and pain during flares.
strategies.notes: Used as needed for symptomatic relief, not for long-term management. Dietary avoidance is the primary approach. Overuse can contribute to constipation.

strategies.name: Loperamide or bismuth subsalicylate (short-term)
strategies.description: Over-the-counter anti-motility or anti-diarrhoeal agents can provide temporary relief of diarrhoea. Bismuth subsalicylate may also reduce gas and pain.
strategies.notes: Use only occasionally during flares, not as routine management. Avoid if symptoms are severe or if there is bloody stool. Can mask underlying problem if overused.

things_to_avoid: Sugar-free gum, mints, and sweets—virtually all contain sorbitol or xylitol.
Diet or 'zero sugar' soft drinks and energy drinks—almost all contain artificial sweeteners including polyols.
High-dose polyol-containing fruits (pears, apples with skin, peaches, plums, avocados, watermelon) unless tolerance is confirmed.
Mushrooms and cauliflower in large quantities—both contain significant polyols.
Processed low-calorie foods marketed as 'guilt-free' or 'sugar-free'—common sources of hidden polyols.
Sugar substitutes like sorbitol, xylitol, and maltitol in cooking, baking, or beverages.
Eating large meals quickly—increases the bolus of unabsorbed carbohydrates entering the colon.
High-fibre intake without adequate hydration—can worsen bloating and gas when combined with polyol fermentation.
Stress and sleep deprivation—both worsen gut barrier function and intolerance symptoms.
Antibiotics (if possible)—can kill beneficial bacteria and worsen dysbiosis, increasing fermentation and symptoms.

key_levers.lever: Consistent polyol avoidance and label reading
key_levers.why_it_matters: This is the only way to prevent symptoms at the source. Without dietary control, medications and supplements provide only temporary symptomatic relief. A single exposure can derail progress and take days to recover.
key_levers.how_to_start: Spend one afternoon reading the labels of your most-eaten packaged foods and swapping obvious culprits (diet drinks, sugar-free snacks, artificial sweeteners). Keep a reference list on your phone of 10–15 safe and unsafe foods.
key_levers.timeline: Symptom improvement within 3–7 days; full stabilisation within 2–4 weeks.

key_levers.lever: Working with a registered dietitian to personalise the diet
key_levers.why_it_matters: A one-size-fits-all avoidance diet can become restrictive and nutritionally inadequate. A dietitian helps identify which polyols matter most for you, what you can tolerate in small amounts, and ensures adequate fibre, micronutrients, and calories.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ask your GP for a referral to a registered dietitian (RD) or search for one specialising in IBS or FODMAP. Many now offer telehealth appointments.
key_levers.timeline: Usually 3–6 sessions over 2–3 months to map tolerance and develop a sustainable plan. Ongoing benefits as you learn to manage the condition.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Stress and poor sleep increase intestinal permeability, worsen dysbiosis, and amplify intolerance symptoms. These are modifiable and have broad health benefits.
key_levers.how_to_start: Choose one stress-reduction practice (e.g. daily 10-minute meditation, evening walks, or breathing exercises) and commit to it for 4 weeks. Simultaneously audit sleep: aim for 7–9 hours, no screens 1 hour before bed, consistent sleep/wake times.
key_levers.timeline: Subjective improvement in bloating and fatigue within 2–4 weeks; more pronounced benefits by 8 weeks.

key_levers.lever: Identifying and treating dysbiosis or secondary gut issues
key_levers.why_it_matters: If dysbiosis, SIBO, or inflammation is present, polyol intolerance symptoms can be severe even with avoidance. Addressing the underlying dysbiosis can restore tolerance and improve overall health.
key_levers.how_to_start: Work with a gastroenterologist or functional medicine practitioner to evaluate for SIBO (breath test), dysbiosis (stool test), or inflammation (fecal calprotectin). If found, treat with targeted antimicrobials, probiotics, or other interventions.
key_levers.timeline: If dysbiosis or SIBO is present and treated, tolerance may improve significantly over 8–12 weeks. If no underlying issue, avoidance remains the sole lever.

key_levers.lever: Gradual expansion of tolerance through planned reintroduction
key_levers.why_it_matters: Most people can eventually tolerate small amounts of polyols if symptoms are first controlled and the gut environment improves. This restores dietary flexibility and quality of life without triggering relapse.
key_levers.how_to_start: After 4+ weeks of strict avoidance and symptom resolution, reintroduce one low-polyol food (e.g. a small slice of apple) at lunch, note symptoms over 2 hours, and record the result. Try a new food every 2–3 days on separate occasions.
key_levers.timeline: Threshold mapping takes 4–8 weeks. Most people identify at least a few foods they can reincorporate in small amounts.

common_mistakes: Assuming that because a food is 'natural' or 'healthy' it is safe—pears, stone fruits, and mushrooms are nutritious but can trigger severe symptoms.
Relying on 'sugar-free' alternatives without reading labels—many contain hidden polyols, perpetuating the problem.
Eliminating all fibre and fermentable foods at once—can lead to constipation and nutritional deficiency. Removal should be targeted and monitored.
Not checking medication labels—some liquid medications, especially for children, contain sorbitol as a sweetener.
Expecting antibiotics or probiotics to 'cure' the problem—avoidance is the primary treatment. Supplements can support but do not replace dietary change.
Reintroducing too many foods at once—makes it impossible to identify which foods are tolerated.
Feeling ashamed or hiding the condition—this can lead to unplanned exposure and hamper social recovery. Being upfront helps friends and family support you.
Cycling between strict avoidance and indulgent relapses—this perpetuates symptoms and dysbiosis. Gradual, planned reintroduction is far more effective.
Assuming that because symptoms improve on low-FODMAP diet, all high-FODMAP foods must be avoided forever—most can be reintroduced in moderation after symptom control.
Neglecting stress and sleep in favour of diet alone—these are equally important drivers of symptom severity.

specialists.role: Essential for personalising polyol avoidance, identifying tolerable threshold levels, and ensuring nutritional adequacy. Can provide one-to-one support for meal planning and label reading.
specialists.notes: Ask for someone with expertise in IBS, FODMAP, or carbohydrate malabsorption. Dietitians are most useful once polyol intolerance is suspected or confirmed; they do not diagnose but help manage.

specialists.role: Can order and interpret breath tests, rule out organic disease, and diagnose secondary conditions like SIBO. Often the first specialist to encounter and formally diagnose polyol intolerance.
specialists.notes: Not all gastroenterologists are familiar with polyol intolerance; ask specifically if they can order sorbitol breath testing. Some are sceptical of FODMAP and functional diagnoses—may need to seek out a more open-minded practitioner.

specialists.role: Takes a systems-based approach, investigates dysbiosis and root causes, and may order comprehensive stool testing or genetic testing. Often more familiar with polyol intolerance and FODMAP concepts.
specialists.notes: These practitioners may be outside the NHS; check credentials. Evidence base varies; ensure they use evidence-based testing and avoid unproven supplements.

specialists.role: Can refer to gastroenterology, dietetics, or functional medicine. Can rule out serious underlying causes (IBD, coeliac disease, etc.) and coordinate care.
specialists.notes: GPs vary in familiarity with polyol intolerance. Providing them with clear information about your symptoms and suspected diagnosis helps prompt appropriate referrals.

patient_communities.name: IBS Network
patient_communities.notes: UK-based charity with resources, forums, and support groups specifically for IBS, which commonly coexists with polyol intolerance. Good starting point for general IBS management and FODMAP information.

patient_communities.name: FODMAP Friendly
patient_communities.notes: Australian resource with a searchable food database, recipes, and information on low-FODMAP eating. Includes specific guidance on polyol-containing foods.

patient_communities.name: r/IBS (subreddit)
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community sharing experiences, tips, and support. Frequently discusses FODMAP and polyol avoidance. Moderated but quality varies; take anecdotes with caution.

patient_communities.name: r/FODMAPS (subreddit)
patient_communities.notes: Dedicated to low-FODMAP diet and FODMAP intolerances, including polyol sensitivity. Active discussion of food challenges, recipes, and coping strategies.

patient_communities.name: FODMAP Friendly Facebook Groups
patient_communities.notes: Various private and public groups focussed on low-FODMAP eating and IBS management. Peer support and real-world recipe sharing; search for groups in your country or region.

patient_communities.name: Digestive Health Alliance
patient_communities.notes: Some chapters or online communities focus on functional GI disorders and food intolerances. Availability varies by region.

learning_resources.title: The Complete Low-FODMAP Diet
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive guide to low-FODMAP eating, including detailed food lists, three-phase approach, and recipes. Covers the science behind FODMAP and practical implementation. Written by the developers of the diet at Monash University.

learning_resources.title: IBS: Free at Last
learning_resources.description: Practical guide to managing IBS using low-FODMAP principles. Includes meal plans, lifestyle advice, and troubleshooting common problems.

learning_resources.title: Monash University Low-FODMAP Diet
learning_resources.description: Official source of FODMAP science and practice. Includes food database, app, research summaries, and practitioner resources. Authoritative and regularly updated.

learning_resources.title: NICE Guideline NG159: Irritable Bowel Syndrome in Adults
learning_resources.author_or_source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE)
learning_resources.description: Evidence-based guideline from NICE on IBS diagnosis and management, including discussion of FODMAP and other dietary approaches. Relevant for understanding how polyol intolerance fits into IBS care.

learning_resources.title: Hydrogen Breath Testing in Gastrointestinal Disorders
learning_resources.description: Technical review of breath testing for detecting carbohydrate malabsorption, including sorbitol and other polyols. Useful for understanding how testing works and its limitations.

learning_resources.title: Polyol Intolerance and FODMAP Sensitivity
learning_resources.description: Many registered dietitian websites provide plain-language explanations and food lists specific to polyol avoidance. Search for 'polyol intolerance' or 'sorbitol intolerance' combined with dietitian resources.

learning_resources.title: Eating Well with IBS: 100 Recipes for a Healthier Digestive System
learning_resources.description: Collection of IBS-friendly recipes that avoid common triggers including polyols. Practical and accessible for meal planning.

learning_resources.title: The Gut Health Doctor
learning_resources.description: Guide to gut health including discussion of food intolerances, dysbiosis, and the low-FODMAP diet. Evidence-based and practical.

learning_resources.title: FODMAP AI App
learning_resources.description: Mobile apps designed to help users navigate low-FODMAP eating, with searchable food databases, meal logging, and recipes. Monash University also has an official app.

learning_resources.title: YouTube: Dietitian reviews of FODMAP and polyol avoidance
learning_resources.description: Search for registered dietitians discussing FODMAP, IBS, or polyol intolerance on YouTube. Visual explanations and real-world food examples can be helpful for learning.

sections.relevance_signal: **Gas, bloating, urgency** after stone fruits (peaches, plums, cherries), sugar-free gum, or diet products containing xylitol or sorbitol.
